```mermaid
    AE1 -->|"Causal link suspected"| AE2
    AE1 -->|"Meets seriousness criteria"| AE3
    AE3 -->|"Unexpected per IB or label"| AE4
    AE4 -->|"Fatal or Life Threatening"| AE5
    AE4 -->|"Other serious events"| AE6

    classDef nodeAE fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#064E3B
    classDef nodeADR fill:#BFDBFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef nodeSAE fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,color:#78350F
    classDef nodeSUSAR fill:#FBCFE8,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843
    classDef nodeReport fill:#FECACA,stroke:#991B1B,color:#7F1D1D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The arrows in this funnel are *conditional* — only *some* AEs become ADRs, only some AEs are serious, etc. The funnel narrows because each subsequent category is more *restrictive*. SUSARs are at the *apex* of regulatory urgency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the following terms with one example each:**
1. **Pharmacokinetics (PK)**
2. **Informed Consent Form (ICF)**
3. **Placebo**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Pharmacokinetics (PK):** Pharmacokinetics is the branch of pharmacology that describes *"what the body does to the drug"* — encompassing the **ADME** processes: **A**bsorption, **D**istribution, **M**etabolism, and **E**xcretion. It is quantified by parameters such as Cmax (peak plasma concentration), Tmax (time to peak), AUC (area under the curve), and t½ (half-life). *Example:* Measuring plasma paracetamol concentration over 24 hours after an oral dose.

2. **Informed Consent Form (ICF):** A written document signed by the trial subject (or legal guardian) *before* any trial-related procedure is performed. It must contain the 8 mandatory elements as per **21 CFR 50.25**: purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, alternatives, confidentiality, compensation for injury, and contact information. It embodies the ethical principle of *autonomy*. *Example:* The signed consent form of a 45-year-old patient enrolled in a diabetes drug trial.

3. **Placebo:** A pharmacologically *inert* substance (e.g., lactose tablet, saline injection) formulated to appear identical to the investigational product. Used as a control to distinguish the *true pharmacological effect* from the *psychological* (placebo) effect. *Example:* Sugar pill used as control in a migraine trial.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between the following pairs in 2–3 lines each:**
1. **AE vs. ADR**
2. **IND vs. NDA**
3. **Phase II vs. Phase III trial**

**Model Answer:**

1. **AE vs. ADR:** An *Adverse Event (AE)* is any untoward medical occurrence in a subject administered a drug — it does **not** imply causality. An *Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR)* is an AE for which a **causal relationship** with the drug is suspected, suspected to be related, or established. All ADRs are AEs, but not all AEs are ADRs.

2. **IND vs. NDA:** An *Investigational New Drug (IND)* application is filed **before** human trials begin (Phase I), requesting regulatory permission to test the drug in humans. A *New Drug Application (NDA)* is filed **after** successful Phase III trials, requesting permission to *market* the drug commercially. IND is the "entry gate," NDA is the "exit gate."

3. **Phase II vs. Phase III:** Phase II (*Therapeutic Exploratory*) involves 100–300 patients and aims to establish **efficacy** and dose-response. Phase III (*Therapeutic Confirmatory / Pivotal*) involves 1,000–10,000+ patients across multiple sites and aims to **confirm efficacy** against the current standard-of-care and detect less common adverse events. Phase III is mandatory for NDA filing; Phase II is not.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Define **Randomization** and **Blinding** in clinical trials. Explain the different types of blinding with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **four phases (I–IV)** of clinical trials in detail, covering objectives, population, duration, and typical endpoints. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Randomization:**
Randomization is the process of allocating subjects to treatment or control groups purely by *chance*, using computer-generated random number tables or validated statistical software. Its primary purpose is to **eliminate selection bias** and ensure that the groups being compared are *comparable* at baseline in both known and unknown confounding variables.

Common methods of randomization include:
- **Simple Randomization:** Each subject is independently assigned by a coin flip or random number generator.
- **Block Randomization:** Subjects are randomized in blocks (e.g., of 4 or 6) to ensure balanced group sizes throughout the trial.
- **Stratified Randomization:** Subjects are first categorized by a prognostic factor (e.g., gender, disease severity) and then randomized within each stratum.

**Blinding (Masking):**
Blinding is a procedure in which one or more parties involved in the trial are kept unaware of the assigned treatment to prevent subjective bias in outcome assessment.

**Types of Blinding:**

| Type | Who is Unaware | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Open-label / Unblinded** | Nobody | Early oncology Phase I |
| **Single-blind** | Subject only | Subject doesn't know if on active or placebo |
| **Double-blind** | Subject + Investigator | Standard Phase III trial — neither party knows treatment |
| **Triple-blind** | Subject + Investigator + Data Analyst | Even the statistician analyzing interim data is blinded |

**Examples:**
- *Single-blind:* A trial testing a new behavioral therapy where the participant doesn't know whether they received the active counseling or the attention-control session.
- *Double-blind:* The classic aspirin vs. placebo cardiovascular trial — both the cardiologist and the patient are unaware until the code is broken after database lock.
- *Triple-blind:* A vaccine trial where the data monitoring committee and the biostatistics team analyzing the immunogenicity endpoints are also blinded.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write *"Double-blind means both eyes are closed"* as a joke, but a more common real mistake is conflating *"double-blind"* with *"triple-blind"* by omitting the data analyst. Always specify the *three* roles in double-blind: subject + investigator (and add statistician for triple). **Lose 1 mark** if you skip the table of types.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Phase I — Human Pharmacology / Safety (0.5–2 years)**
- **Population:** 20–100 *healthy* volunteers (exceptions: oncology, AIDS — where patients are used due to toxicity or ethical reasons).
- **Objective:** Determine *safety*, *tolerability*, *maximum tolerated dose (MTD)*, and basic *pharmacokinetic* parameters.
- **Sub-types:** SAD (Single Ascending Dose) and MAD (Multiple Ascending Dose) studies. Phase 0 / Microdosing uses sub-pharmacological doses (≤100 μg) for early human PK.
- **Endpoints:** Dose-limiting toxicities (DLT), Cmax, Tmax, AUC, t½.

**Phase II — Therapeutic Exploratory (1–3 years)**
- **Population:** 100–300 *patients* with the target disease.
- **Objective:** Establish *proof-of-concept* and determine the *optimal dose*.
- **Sub-types:** Phase IIa (pilot efficacy) and Phase IIb (dose-finding).
- **Endpoints:** Clinical improvement, biomarker changes, dose-response curve, Therapeutic Index (TI).

**Phase III — Therapeutic Confirmatory / Pivotal (2–6 years)**
- **Population:** 1,000–10,000+ patients, often across dozens of countries and hundreds of sites.
- **Objective:** *Confirm* efficacy, monitor less-common adverse events, compare with the *standard-of-care (SOC)*.
- **Designs:** Randomized, double-blind, parallel-group, active-controlled, often with a placebo arm if ethically permissible.
- **Endpoints:** Statistically powered primary endpoint (e.g., mortality, morbidity, validated symptom score).
- **Output:** Forms the core dataset for NDA/MAA submission.

**Phase IV — Post-Marketing Surveillance (ongoing, post-launch)**
- **Population:** The general patient population, often in real-world clinical settings.
- **Objective:** Detect *rare adverse events* (1 in 10,000+), long-term safety, drug interactions, use in special populations (pediatric, geriatric, pregnant).
- **Regulatory Trigger:** Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs), Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies (REMS).
- **Examples:** Rofecoxib (Vioxx) was withdrawn in 2004 based on Phase IV data showing increased cardiovascular risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "Phase III is the last phase" and skip Phase IV entirely. This is a **2-mark deduction** in standard KTU marking schemes. Also, students often confuse Phase I being done in *patients* vs. *healthy volunteers* — **always qualify with the exception** (oncology/AIDS trials use patients from Phase I).

---

### **Question B — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the role of the **Sponsor, CRO, IRB/IEC, and DSMB** in a clinical trial. Describe how the data flows from subject to the final regulatory submission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define and distinguish between **Bioavailability** and **Bioequivalence**. Derive the expression for absolute oral bioavailability from AUC data. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Sponsor** — The sponsor is the *legal entity* (pharma/biotech company, government agency, or individual) that takes overall responsibility for the trial. Responsibilities include: trial design, regulatory submissions (IND/CTA), funding, selection of investigators and CROs, pharmacovigilance, and final NDA preparation.

**CRO (Contract Research Organization)** — The CRO is a *third-party* organization contracted by the sponsor to perform trial-related duties. Tasks outsourced include: site monitoring, data management, biostatistics, regulatory affairs, medical writing, central laboratory services. *Examples: IQVIA, Parexel, Syneos Health.* Using a CRO reduces sponsor overhead and provides access to global expertise.

**IRB / IEC (Institutional Review Board / Independent Ethics Committee)** — An *independent* committee of ≥5 members (medical, scientific, and non-scientific, with at least one independent layperson) that reviews the trial's *ethical* aspects: scientific validity, risk–benefit ratio, subject protection measures, ICF contents. The IRB/IEC approves the trial *before* it starts, conducts *continuing reviews*, and has authority to *suspend or terminate* the trial at any site.

**DSMB (Data Safety Monitoring Board)** — An *independent* expert committee that periodically reviews *unblinded* interim safety data, especially in Phase III trials. The DSMB can recommend to the sponsor that the trial be **continued**, **modified**, or **stopped early** for safety, futility, or overwhelming efficacy.

**Data Flow (Subject → Regulatory Submission):**

$$\text{Subject Visit} \rightarrow \text{CRF Entry by CRC} \rightarrow \text{Source Data Verification by CRA} \rightarrow \text{Database Lock} \rightarrow \text{Statistical Analysis by Biostatistics Team} \rightarrow \text{Clinical Study Report (CSR)} \rightarrow \text{Integrated Summary of Safety and Efficacy} \rightarrow \text{NDA / MAA Module 5 Dossier} \rightarrow \text{Regulatory Authority (FDA / EMA / CDSCO)}$$

**Step-by-Step:**
1. Subject visits site → clinical data and samples collected.
2. CRC enters data into **eCRF** (electronic Case Report Form).
3. CRA monitors site → **SDV** (Source Data Verification) against medical records.
4. After all subjects complete, database is **locked** (no further edits).
5. Biostatisticians run pre-specified analyses per **Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP)**.
6. Medical writers compile **Clinical Study Report (CSR)** following ICH-E3 format.
7. Sponsor integrates multiple CSRs into **Module 5** of the eCTD submission.
8. NDA/MAA filed with regulatory authority → review by medical officers → approval/rejection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not list CRO and Sponsor as the *same* entity. The CRO is a *service provider* — it has no legal liability for the trial's outcome (the sponsor does). Also, the IRB/IEC operates *independently* of the sponsor and CRO. Saying *"the sponsor's IRB"* will lose you marks. **Common deduction: 2 marks.**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Definitions:**

- **Bioavailability (F):** The rate and extent to which the *active ingredient* is absorbed from a drug product and becomes available at the *site of action*. For oral drugs, it is the fraction of the oral dose that reaches systemic circulation unchanged.

- **Bioequivalence:** Two drug products are bioequivalent if they exhibit the *same rate and extent of absorption* when administered at the same molar dose under similar conditions. Regulatory acceptance criteria: the 90% confidence interval of the ratio (Test/Reference) for both **AUC** and **Cmax** must fall within **80%–125%**.

**Distinguishing Table:**

| Parameter | Bioavailability | Bioequivalence |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Compares | Drug formulation vs. IV route | Two formulations of the *same* drug |
| Outcome | Absolute or relative (%) | Comparative (Test vs. Reference) |
| Required for | Generic drug development *only if* absolute BA cannot be measured | All generic AND branded formulation changes |
| Acceptance | Variable (depends on drug) | 90% CI of ratio within 80–125% |

**Derivation of Absolute Oral Bioavailability:**

Given:
- IV bolus dose $D_{IV}$, AUC = $AUC_{IV}$
- Oral dose $D_{oral}$, AUC = $AUC_{oral}$

By the principle of pharmacokinetics, the AUC is given by:

$$AUC = \frac{F \times D}{Cl}$$

For the IV route, $F_{IV} = 1$:

$$AUC_{IV} = \frac{1 \times D_{IV}}{Cl} \implies Cl = \frac{D_{IV}}{AUC_{IV}}$$

For the oral route, $F_{oral}$ is unknown:

$$AUC_{oral} = \frac{F_{oral} \times D_{oral}}{Cl}$$

Substituting $Cl$ from the IV equation:

$$AUC_{oral} = \frac{F_{oral} \times D_{oral}}{D_{IV} / AUC_{IV}}$$

$$\implies AUC_{oral} = \frac{F_{oral} \times D_{oral} \times AUC_{IV}}{D_{IV}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{F_{oral} = \frac{AUC_{oral} \times D_{IV}}{AUC_{IV} \times D_{oral}}}$$

**Verification:** If the oral drug is 100% absorbed and not metabolized, $AUC_{oral} = AUC_{IV}$ for equal doses, giving $F = 1$. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the formula in the wrong direction (e.g., $F = \frac{AUC_{IV} \times D_{oral}}{AUC_{oral} \times D_{IV}}$). The numerator **must** contain $AUC_{oral}$ and $D_{IV}$ — remember: *Oral on top, IV on bottom in the AUC ratio*. **Lose 2 marks** for inversion. Also, units must be consistent (e.g., both doses in mg, both AUCs in ng·hr/mL).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Clinical Trial Terminologies**

### Core Phases (Mnemonic: "S-E-C-P" — Safety, Efficacy, Confirm, Post)
- Phase I → **S**afety & PK in *healthy* volunteers (20–100).
- Phase II → **E**fficacy in patients (100–300).
- Phase III → **C**onfirmatory in large populations (1k–10k).
- Phase IV → **P**ost-marketing surveillance (real-world).

### Personnel Mnemonic — "S-C-P-I-D"
- **S**ponsor (legal, financial)
- **C**RO (outsourced operations)
- **P**I (on-site medical)
- **I**RB/IEC (ethics)
- **D**SMB (safety monitoring)

### Design Mnemonic — "R-B-P-C"
- **R**andomization (chance allocation → no selection bias)
- **B**linding (hiding identity → no observation bias)
- **P**lacebo (inert control → no placebo effect bias)
- **C**ontrol group (benchmark → comparative efficacy)

### Documentation Hierarchy — "P-C-I-B"
- **P**rotocol (law of the trial)
- **C**RF (data capture form)
- **I**nvestigator's Brochure (background science)
- **I**nformed Consent (subject's autonomy)

### Safety Escalation Funnel
AE → ADR → SAE → SUSAR (7 days fatal, 15 days other).

### Key Regulatory Documents
- **IND** (US, pre-trial) → FDA
- **CTA** (EU, pre-trial) → EMA
- **NDA** (US, post-trial) → FDA
- **MAA** (EU, post-trial) → EMA
- **CTRI** (India, registration) → ICMR

### Pharmacology Quick-Recall
- **PK** = ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion).
- **PD** = Mechanism of action + dose-response.
- **Bioavailability** = Fraction reaching systemic circulation.
- **Bioequivalence** = Same rate and extent; 90% CI of ratio in 80–125%.

### Numericals to Always Remember
- $F = \dfrac{AUC_{oral} \times D_{IV}}{AUC_{IV} \times D_{oral}}$
- $TI = \dfrac{TD_{50}}{ED_{50}}$
- Bioequivalence window: **0.80 to 1.25** at 90% CI.
- SUSAR reporting: **7 days** (fatal), **15 days** (other SAE).
- Phase I population: **20–100**; Phase III population: **1,000–10,000+**.

### Three Pillars of Every Clinical Trial
1. **Scientific Rigor** (valid design, randomization, blinding)
2. **Ethical Integrity** (ICF, IRB approval, subject welfare)
3. **Regulatory Compliance** (Schedule Y, 21 CFR, EU CTR, ICH-GCP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** Before writing any KTU answer on this topic, *categorize* the question into one of the seven (7) categories from Section 1 (Regulatory, Personnel, Design, Documentation, Ethical, Pharmacological, Safety). This immediately structures your answer and earns the *organization* marks that KTU examiners allocate (typically 1–2 marks per 14-mark answer).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPCSEA Guidelines and Pre-clinical Trials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT634 - CLINICAL RESEARCH AND DRUG DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CPCSEA Guidelines and Pre-clinical Trials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 CPCSEA — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPCSEA (Committee for the Purpose of Control and Supervision of Experiments on Animals)** is a statutory body constituted by the **Government of India** under the **Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960** (specifically under **Section 15** and **Rule 17** of the **Breeding of and Experiments on Animals (Control and Supervision) Rules, 1998**, as amended in **2001 and 2006**). Its primary mandate is to regulate the use of animals in research and education throughout India and to ensure that animal experimentation is conducted under strict ethical, scientific, and humane guidelines.

The CPCSEA functions under the **Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry and Dairying**, Government of India, with its registered office at the **National Institute of Animal Welfare (NIAW)**, Ballabhgarh, Haryana. It is the apex regulatory authority that oversees the activities of **Institutional Animal Ethics Committees (IAECs)** across all universities, research institutes, pharmaceutical companies, and biotechnology firms in India.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Historical Note for KTU Exams:** CPCSEA was reconstituted in 2001 with expanded powers following the Supreme Court directive in *WPA 250/2000* (People for Ethical Treatment of Animals vs. Union of India), which mandated stricter oversight of animal experiments in India.

---

### 1.2 Pre-clinical Trials — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-clinical trials** (also termed *pre-clinical studies* or *non-clinical studies*) are the rigorous, controlled laboratory investigations conducted *in vitro* (test tubes, cell cultures) and *in vivo* (living animals) **before** a new chemical entity (NCE) or investigational new drug (IND) is administered to humans for the first time. These trials generate the foundational **safety, efficacy, pharmacokinetic (PK), pharmacodynamic (PD), and toxicological data** required for regulatory submission (Investigational New Drug application to **CDSCO / DCGI** in India, or IND to **USFDA**).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Analogy)

**Analogy 1 — The "Car Crash Test Dummy" Analogy:**

Imagine a car manufacturer has designed a brand-new prototype vehicle. Before letting any human drive it on real roads, the company must:
- Crash-test it on **dummy mannequins** (analogous to **animal models**)
- Run computer simulations (analogous to **in vitro assays**)
- Check fuel efficiency, braking, and structural integrity (analogous to **pharmacodynamics & pharmacokinetics**)
- Submit engineering blueprints to a regulatory body like the **Motor Vehicles Department** (analogous to **CPCSEA / DCGI / FDA**)

> If any dummy is severely damaged or dies during testing, the company is **not permitted** to put the car on sale for public use until the issue is fixed.

**Exactly the same logic applies to drug development:**
- Animals = "biological crash-test dummies" for humans
- Pre-clinical trials = The complete engineering and safety assessment of the prototype
- CPCSEA = The Indian regulatory body ensuring that no animal is subjected to unnecessary or unethical suffering during this "crash test"

**Analogy 2 — The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy for Drug Pipeline:**

Think of the drug development process like a high-end restaurant kitchen:
- **Discovery (idea stage)** = Brainstorming a new dish recipe
- **Pre-clinical trials** = Testing the recipe on internal staff and food critics (not paying customers yet!)
- **Phase I Clinical Trial** = Serving a small portion to a few trusted, paid volunteers (safety check)
- **Phase II Clinical Trial** = Limited menu launch to gauge customer interest (efficacy + side effects)
- **Phase III Clinical Trial** = Full restaurant launch (large-scale efficacy and comparative studies)
- **Phase IV / Post-Marketing** = Monitoring customer reviews and complaints after launch

In this analogy, **CPCSEA** acts like the **Food Safety Officer** who ensures the kitchen staff treats its test tasters (animals) ethically and humanely during the recipe-testing phase.

---

### 1.4 Core Mandate Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Pillars of This Module:**
> 1. **CPCSEA Guidelines** → *"How" to conduct animal research ethically and lawfully in India*
> 2. **Pre-clinical Trials** → *"What" experiments to perform and what data to generate before human testing*

Both pillars together ensure that the **translational bridge** from laboratory bench to bedside (often called "**bench-to-bedside**" transition) is built on a foundation of **scientific rigor, ethical integrity, and regulatory compliance**.

---

### 1.5 Physical / Standard Metrics Snapshot

| Parameter | Standard Metric / Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| CPCSEA Main Committee size | **28 members** (approx.) | Chaired by a senior bureaucrat |
| Quorum for IAEC meeting | **Minimum 4 members** | Including 1 external member |
| IAEC approval required for | Every animal experiment protocol | Mandatory before initiation |
| Pre-clinical study duration (typical) | **2 to 6 years** | Depends on therapeutic area |
| Animal-to-human dose conversion factor (mouse to human) | **~12.3** | FDA Guidance, 2005 |
| Animal-to-human dose conversion factor (rat to human) | **~6.2** | FDA Guidance, 2005 |
| Pre-clinical attrition rate | **~90%** of candidates fail | Industry statistic |
| OECD GLP certification validity | Reviewed **annually** | National GLP Compliance Programme |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CPCSEA — Composition, Constitution & Governance

#### 2.1.1 Hierarchical Structure

CPCSEA operates through a **three-tier hierarchical structure**:

**Tier 1 — The Central CPCSEA (Apex Body)**
- Headed by a **Chairperson** (typically a senior IAS officer or distinguished scientist)
- Includes representatives from:
  - **Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry and Dairying** (nodal ministry)
  - **Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR)**
  - **Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR)**
  - **Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR)**
  - **Drug Controller General of India (DCGI)**
  - **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)**
  - National experts in pharmacology, toxicology, veterinary science, ethics
  - **Non-governmental animal welfare organisations** (e.g., PETA India, SPCA)
  - **Independent scientific experts** from universities and research institutes

**Tier 2 — Institutional Animal Ethics Committees (IAECs)**
- Constituted at every institution performing animal experiments
- **Mandatory** for every institution before any animal experiment
- Composition (typically 8 members):
  1. A **biological scientist** (Chairperson)
  2. A **scientist from a different biological discipline**
  3. A **veterinarian** (specialized in laboratory animal medicine)
  4. The **institutional scientist-in-charge of animal house**
  5. A **scientist from outside the institute** (external member, mandatory)
  6. A **non-scientific socially aware member**
  7. A **maintenance person** (in-charge of animal house)
  8. A **Member Secretary** (typically the in-charge scientist)

**Tier 3 — The Institutional Animal House Facility**
- The physical location where animals are bred, housed, and experimented upon
- Must comply with CPCSEA standards for **cage size, temperature, humidity, light cycle, ventilation, food, water, and bedding**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common short-answer question asks: *"What is the minimum number of IAEC members required for a valid meeting?"* — The answer is **5 members**, with at least one **external (non-institutional) member** to constitute quorum.

---

#### 2.1.2 Functions of CPCSEA (High-Yield for KTU)

The CPCSEA performs the following statutory functions:

1. **Approval and Registration** of Institutions conducting animal experiments
2. **Approval of Animal House Facilities** — registration is mandatory before operating
3. **Review and Approval** of all experimental protocols through IAECs
4. **Inspection** of animal facilities periodically (announced and unannounced)
5. **Suspension or Cancellation** of registration in case of violations
6. **Recommendations** to the Central Government on policy matters related to animal welfare
7. **Promotion of Alternatives** — actively encourages the **3Rs principle**
8. **Training and Capacity Building** of researchers, veterinarians, and animal care staff
9. **Maintenance of a National Database** of institutions, IAECs, and approved protocols
10. **Disciplinary Action** against institutions violating guidelines

---

#### 2.1.3 The 3Rs Principle — The Ethical Cornerstone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3Rs Principle** (proposed by **William Russell and Rex Burch in 1959** in their landmark book *"The Principles of Humane Experimental Technique"*) is the philosophical foundation of CPCSEA's animal welfare policy.

| R | Full Form | Meaning | Practical Example in Drug Discovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Replacement** | Use of non-animal methods wherever possible | Replacing live animals with *in vitro* cell lines, organoids, computer simulations, or *ex vivo* tissues | Using human liver microsomes for CYP450 enzyme studies instead of rat liver perfusion |
| **Reduction** | Minimizing the number of animals used | Better experimental design, statistical power analysis, shared control groups, *in silico* screening before animal studies | Using **10 rats** instead of 50 by careful statistical power calculation |
| **Refinement** | Minimizing pain, distress, and suffering | Better anaesthesia, analgesia, humane endpoints, improved housing, environmental enrichment | Using ultrasound-guided blood sampling instead of retro-orbital puncture to avoid retinal damage |

> **KTU High-Yield:** The 3Rs are often asked as a 5-mark short essay. Memorize the definition of each R with **one pharmaceutical example** each.

---

### 2.2 Pre-clinical Trials — Deep Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 Objectives of Pre-clinical Studies

The primary objectives of pre-clinical trials are:

1. **Pharmacodynamic (PD) Evaluation** — *What does the drug do to the body?*
   - Mechanism of action
   - Receptor binding studies
   - Dose-response relationship
   - *In vitro* and *ex vivo* assays

2. **Pharmacokinetic (PK) Evaluation** — *What does the body do to the drug?*
   - **ADME**: Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion
   - Bioavailability
   - Plasma half-life
   - Tissue distribution
   - Routes of administration comparison

3. **Safety / Toxicology Evaluation** — *Is the drug safe at therapeutic and supra-therapeutic doses?*
   - Acute toxicity
   - Sub-acute (sub-chronic) toxicity
   - Chronic toxicity
   - Genotoxicity / Mutagenicity
   - Reproductive toxicity
   - Carcinogenicity
   - Local tolerance

4. **Formulation Development** — Developing a stable, bioavailable dosage form

5. **Regulatory Documentation** — Preparing the IND dossier (Investigational New Drug application)

---

#### 2.2.2 Phases of Pre-clinical Trials

Pre-clinical trials are broadly classified into **three phases**:

**Phase 1 — Discovery & Screening (Basic Research)**
- Target identification
- High-throughput screening (HTS) of thousands of compounds
- *In vitro* assays
- Hit-to-lead optimization
- Duration: **1–3 years**
- Cost: ~$10–50 million
- Output: Lead compound with desirable PD profile

**Phase 2 — Lead Optimization & IND-Enabling Studies**
- Medicinal chemistry optimization (ADME properties)
- *In vivo* efficacy studies in animal disease models
- Dose-ranging studies
- PK/PD modeling
- Toxicology studies (acute, sub-acute, genotoxicity)
- Duration: **1–2 years**
- Cost: ~$50–100 million
- Output: Candidate drug (IND candidate)

**Phase 3 — Regulatory Toxicology / IND Submission**
- Full GLP-compliant toxicology package
- 28-day, 90-day, and chronic toxicity studies
- 2-species (rodent + non-rodent) carcinogenicity bioassays
- Reproductive and developmental toxicology
- Safety pharmacology core battery (CV, CNS, respiratory)
- Duration: **1–2 years**
- Cost: ~$100–200 million
- Output: Approved IND application → First-in-Human (FIH) Phase 1 clinical trial

---

#### 2.2.3 Types of Toxicity Studies (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Study Type | Duration | Number of Animals (per group, typical) | Purpose | Regulatory Guideline |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acute Toxicity (Single Dose)** | Single dose, observation 14 days | 5M + 5F per dose, 4 doses | Determine LD50, identify target organs | OECD TG 401 (replaced by 420, 423, 425) |
| **Sub-acute / Sub-chronic (28-day)** | 28 days repeated dosing | 5–10M + 5–10F per dose, 4 doses | Identify NOAEL, target organs | OECD TG 407 |
| **Sub-chronic (90-day)** | 90 days repeated dosing | 10–15M + 10–15F per dose, 4 doses | Establish dose-response for repeated exposure | OECD TG 408 |
| **Chronic Toxicity (6–12 months)** | 6–12 months | 20–30M + 20–30F per dose | Long-term safety profile | OECD TG 452 |
| **Genotoxicity (Mutagenicity)** | In vitro + in vivo | Variable | Detect DNA damage, gene mutations | OECD TG 471 (Ames), 474, 475 |
| **Reproductive Toxicity** | Multi-generational | Variable | Effects on fertility, pregnancy, fetal development | OECD TG 415, 416, 421, 422 |
| **Carcinogenicity** | 24 months (rodent lifespan) | 50M + 50F per dose | Tumorigenic potential | OECD TG 451 |
| **Local Tolerance** | Single / repeated | Variable | Irritation at site of administration | OECD TG 404, 410 |

---

#### 2.2.4 Good Laboratory Practice (GLP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GLP (Good Laboratory Practice)** is a quality system concerned with the **organisational process and conditions** under which non-clinical health and environmental safety studies are planned, performed, monitored, recorded, reported, and archived.

In India, GLP compliance is overseen by the **National GLP Compliance Monitoring Authority (NGCMA)** under the **Department of Science and Technology (DST), Government of India**. The Indian GLP program is **mutually recognised with OECD GLP** since **2013**.

**Key GLP Principles:**
1. **Personnel** — qualified, trained, with documented responsibilities
2. **Facilities** — adequate size, separation of activities, environmental control
3. **Equipment** — calibrated, validated, properly maintained
4. **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** — written for every critical activity
5. **Test Articles and Reference Items** — characterised, stored properly
6. **Study Plans (Protocols)** — written, approved, amendments documented
7. **Raw Data** — recorded directly, legibly, indelibly
8. **Final Reports** — comprehensive, signed by Study Director
9. **Quality Assurance Unit (QAU)** — independent monitoring
10. **Archives** — long-term storage of all records and samples

---

#### 2.2.5 IND (Investigational New Drug) Application

Once pre-clinical studies are complete, the sponsor submits an **IND application** to regulatory authorities:

| Authority | Region | Application Type |
|---|---|---|
| **USFDA (United States)** | USA | **IND** (Investigational New Drug Application) |
| **EMA (European Medicines Agency)** | EU | **CTA** (Clinical Trial Application) / IMPD (Investigational Medicinal Product Dossier) |
| **DCGI (Drugs Controller General of India) / CDSCO** | India | **IND** (Form 44 under Schedule Y) |
| **PMDA** | Japan | IND Application |
| **NMPA** | China | IND Application |

The IND dossier typically includes:
1. **CMC (Chemistry, Manufacturing & Controls)** information
2. **Pre-clinical pharmacology and toxicology data**
3. **Pharmacokinetic and ADME data**
4. **Manufacturing information**
5. **Investigator's Brochure (IB)**
6. **Clinical trial protocol (Phase 1)**

---

#### 2.2.6 Animal Models in Drug Discovery (KTU High-Yield)

| Animal Model | Common Application | Why This Model? |
|---|---|---|
| **Mouse (Mus musculus)** | Cancer, immunology, genetics | Short lifespan, well-characterized genome, transgenic tools available |
| **Rat (Rattus norvegicus)** | Cardiovascular, diabetes, neuropharmacology | Larger size for surgery, well-established disease models (Zucker rat, SHR rat) |
| **Zebrafish (Danio rerio)** | Developmental biology, high-throughput screening | Transparent embryos, ~70% genetic homology with humans, low cost |
| **Dog (Beagle)** | Cardiovascular, acute toxicity (non-rodent) | Hemodynamic similarity to humans |
| **Rabbit (Oryctolagus cuniculus)** | Ocular, dermal, atherosclerosis studies | Large eye for ophthalmology, sensitive skin for irritation testing |
| **Guinea Pig** | Asthma, anaphylaxis, dermal sensitization | Highly sensitive histamine response |
| **Non-human Primate (Cynomolgus / Rhesus Macaque)** | HIV, HCV, monoclonal antibodies, gene therapy | Closest genetic/immunological similarity to humans |
| **Xenopus (Frog)** | Developmental, teratology | Large oocytes for embryology studies |
| **Caenorhabditis elegans (nematode worm)** | Aging, neurodegeneration, HTS | Fully mapped connectome, 959 cells |
| **Drosophila melanogaster (fruit fly)** | Neurodegeneration, longevity | 75% disease gene homologs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** In India, CPCSEA has issued specific guidance that **non-human primates (NHPs)** can only be used when no other validated alternative exists, and requires **special justification and approval** at the central CPCSEA level (not just IAEC).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are essential for any numerical/calculation-based questions on pre-clinical trials. Memorize these for KTU exams.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Litchfield-Wilcoxon Method** for LD50 estimation | $\text{LD}_{50} = \text{antilog}\left[\log D_m - d \cdot \Sigma (r_i)\right]$ | $D_m$ = geometric mean of doses, $d$ = log dose interval, $r_i$ = proportional responses | Acute toxicity LD50 determination |
| 2 | **Mouse-to-Human Dose Conversion** (FDA Guidance 2005) | $\text{HED (mg/kg)} = \text{Mouse Dose (mg/kg)} \times \frac{1}{12.3}$ | HED = Human Equivalent Dose | Translating mouse dose to human starting dose |
| 3 | **Rat-to-Human Dose Conversion** | $\text{HED (mg/kg)} = \text{Rat Dose (mg/kg)} \times \frac{1}{6.2}$ | — | Translating rat dose to human dose |
| 4 | **Dog-to-Human Dose Conversion** | $\text{HED (mg/kg)} = \text{Dog Dose (mg/kg)} \times \frac{1}{1.8}$ | — | Translating dog dose to human dose |
| 5 | **Body Surface Area (BSA) Conversion** | $\text{HED (mg)} = \text{Animal Dose (mg/kg)} \times \text{Animal }K_m \times \frac{\text{Human Weight (kg)}}{\text{Human }K_m}$ | $K_m$ = species-specific scaling factor (mouse=3, rat=6, dog=20, human=37) | FDA-recommended allometric conversion |
| 6 | **Safety Margin / Therapeutic Index** | $\text{TI} = \frac{\text{LD}_{50}}{\text{ED}_{50}}$ or $\text{TI} = \frac{\text{TD}_{50}}{\text{ED}_{50}}$ | LD = lethal dose, ED = effective dose, TD = toxic dose | Wide TI = safer drug |
| 7 | **NOAEL Determination** | $\text{NOAEL} = \text{Highest dose at which no adverse effect is observed}$ | mg/kg/day | Used to calculate Reference Dose (RfD) |
| 8 | **Reference Dose (RfD) / Acceptable Daily Intake** | $\text{RfD} = \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{UF}_1 \times \text{UF}_2 \times \text{UF}_3 \times \text{UF}_4 \times \text{UF}_5}$ | UF = Uncertainty Factors (each typically 10×, max 10,000×) | Safe human exposure limit calculation |
| 9 | **Plasma Half-Life** | $t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k_e}$ | $k_e$ = elimination rate constant | Determines dosing frequency |
| 10 | **Bioavailability (F)** | $F = \frac{\text{AUC}_{\text{extravenous}}}{\text{AUC}_{\text{intravenous}}} \times 100\%$ | AUC = Area Under the Curve | Compares routes of administration |
| 11 | **Volume of Distribution** | $V_d = \frac{\text{Total Drug in Body}}{C_p}$ | $V_d$ in L/kg, $C_p$ = plasma concentration | Predicts drug distribution |
| 12 | **Clearance** | $CL = k_e \times V_d$ | mL/min/kg | Eliminatory capacity |
| 13 | **Pharmacokinetic Power Model (Bodyweight Scaling)** | $\text{Dose}_{\text{human}} = \text{Dose}_{\text{animal}} \times \left(\frac{W_{\text{human}}}{W_{\text{animal}}}\right)^{0.67}$ | $W$ = body weight in kg | Allometric scaling across species |
| 14 | **Cmax and Tmax** | $\text{C}_{\max} = \text{Peak plasma concentration}$, $\text{T}_{\max} = \text{time to reach Cmax}$ | — | Read directly from concentration-time curve |
| 15 | **Steady-State Concentration** | $C_{ss} = \frac{F \times \text{Dose}}{CL \times \tau}$ | $\tau$ = dosing interval | Predicts drug accumulation |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Pharmaceutical Fields

The CPCSEA framework and pre-clinical trial methodology have far-reaching applications:

1. **Pharmaceutical Industry** — every new drug marketed globally has passed through CPCSEA-approved (or equivalent) animal studies
2. **Biotechnology** — monoclonal antibodies, gene therapies, vaccines all require rigorous pre-clinical safety
3. **Medical Device Industry** — biocompatibility testing of implants (ISO 10993) uses animal models
4. **Cosmetics Industry** — animal testing of cosmetics is **banned in India since 2013** and across the EU since 2013 (this is a frequently asked KTU question!)
5. **Pesticide / Agrochemical Industry** — environmental and mammalian toxicology
6. **Regulatory Affairs** — professionals specialize in compiling pre-clinical dossiers
7. **Academic Research** — biomedical research, basic science investigations
8. **Contract Research Organisations (CROs)** — provide GLP-compliant pre-clinical testing services

> [!NOTE]
> **India banned cosmetics testing on animals in 2013.** The Drugs and Cosmetics (Amendment) Rules, 2018, **further prohibited the import of cosmetics tested on animals abroad**. This is a frequently tested KTU factual question.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — Allometric Dose Scaling (Body Surface Area Method)

This is the **most important calculation** in pre-clinical trials and is frequently tested in KTU exams.

#### Problem Statement (Sample KTU Exam Problem):
A new anti-cancer drug **Oncostat-X** has shown efficacy in rat xenograft tumor models at a dose of **50 mg/kg**. A clinical pharmacologist needs to estimate the **starting human dose** for a Phase 1 trial in a **60 kg adult patient**.

**Given Information:**
- Rat dose: 50 mg/kg
- Patient weight: 60 kg
- Species-specific $K_m$ values:
  - Rat $K_m$ = 6
  - Human $K_m$ = 37

---

**Step 1: Recall the FDA-Recommended BSA Scaling Formula**

$$
\text{HED (mg/kg)} = \text{Animal Dose (mg/kg)} \times \frac{\text{Animal } K_m}{\text{Human } K_m}
$$

The conceptual basis is that **metabolic rate scales with body surface area**, not body weight. Therefore, doses must be normalized to BSA using species-specific scaling factors ($K_m$).

---

**Step 2: Substitute the Known Values**

$$
\text{HED (mg/kg)} = 50 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \times \frac{6}{37}
$$

---

**Step 3: Perform the Division**

$$
\frac{6}{37} = 0.1622
$$

Then,

$$
\text{HED (mg/kg)} = 50 \times 0.1622 = 8.11 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}}
$$

---

**Step 4: Calculate the Absolute Dose for the Patient**

$$
\text{Absolute Dose (mg)} = \text{HED (mg/kg)} \times \text{Patient Weight (kg)}
$$

$$
\text{Absolute Dose (mg)} = 8.11 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \times 60 \, \text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Absolute Dose (mg)} = 486.6 \, \text{mg}
$$

---

**Step 5: Apply the Standard Safety Reduction Factor**

By convention, the **starting human dose in Phase 1 oncology trials** is typically set at **1/10th of the HED** calculated from animal studies (this is the FDA's standard recommendation for cytotoxic agents):

$$
\text{Safe Starting Dose (mg)} = \frac{486.6}{10} = 48.66 \, \text{mg}
$$

---

**Step 6: Final Answer with Clinical Interpretation**

$$
\boxed{\text{Recommended Phase 1 starting dose} \approx 48.66 \, \text{mg, rounded to } 50 \, \text{mg}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The starting dose of 50 mg (administered once every 3 weeks) is recommended for the Phase 1 trial. The dose will then be escalated in cohorts of 3–6 patients using a **3+3 dose-escalation design** until the **Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)** is reached.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough — Therapeutic Index (TI) Calculation

#### Problem Statement:
A new antihypertensive drug **Tensocare** has the following data:
- LD50 in mice = 500 mg/kg
- ED50 in spontaneously hypertensive rats = 5 mg/kg

Calculate the Therapeutic Index.

---

**Step 1: Recall the Therapeutic Index Formula**

$$
\text{TI} = \frac{\text{LD}_{50}}{\text{ED}_{50}}
$$

A higher TI indicates a **wider safety margin**.

---

**Step 2: Substitute the Values**

$$
\text{TI} = \frac{500 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}}}{5 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}}}
$$

---

**Step 3: Compute the Ratio**

$$
\text{TI} = 100
$$

---

**Step 4: Interpretation**

$$
\boxed{\text{TI} = 100}
$$

A TI of **100 is excellent** (most marketed drugs have TI > 100). For reference:
- TI < 1 → Dangerous (e.g., warfarin)
- TI = 1–10 → Narrow margin (e.g., digoxin, lithium)
- TI > 100 → Wide margin (e.g., paracetamol, penicillin)

---

### 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — Calculating the No Observed Adverse Effect Level (NOAEL) and Reference Dose

#### Problem Statement:
In a 90-day rat toxicity study of drug **Cardiostop**, the following results were obtained:

| Dose (mg/kg/day) | Observed Effect |
|---|---|
| 0 | No adverse effects |
| 10 | No adverse effects |
| 30 | No adverse effects |
| 100 | Mild hepatic enzyme elevation (ALT, AST) |
| 300 | Severe hepatic necrosis, mortality in 20% |

---

**Step 1: Identify the NOAEL**

The NOAEL is the **highest dose at which no adverse effect is observed**.

Examining the data, the dose of 30 mg/kg/day is the highest dose showing **no adverse effects**. The 100 mg/kg/day dose showed mild hepatic enzyme elevation, which **is** an adverse effect.

Therefore,

$$
\boxed{\text{NOAEL} = 30 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \cdot \text{day}}
$$

---

**Step 2: Apply Uncertainty Factors (UFs) to Calculate the Reference Dose (RfD)**

The RfD formula uses 5 uncertainty factors, each defaulting to **10×** unless scientifically justified otherwise:

$$
\text{RfD} = \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{UF}_1 \times \text{UF}_2 \times \text{UF}_3 \times \text{UF}_4 \times \text{UF}_5}
$$

Where each UF addresses a specific source of uncertainty:

- $\text{UF}_1$ = Interspecies variability (animal to human) = **10**
- $\text{UF}_2$ = Intraspecies variability (human to human) = **10**
- $\text{UF}_3$ = Subchronic to chronic extrapolation = **10**
- $\text{UF}_4$ = LOAEL to NOAEL extrapolation = **1** (since NOAEL is available)
- $\text{UF}_5$ = Database incompleteness = **1** (if database is otherwise complete)

---

**Step 3: Calculate the RfD**

$$
\text{RfD} = \frac{30 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \cdot \text{day}}{10 \times 10 \times 10 \times 1 \times 1}
$$

$$
\text{RfD} = \frac{30 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \cdot \text{day}}{1000}
$$

$$
\text{RfD} = 0.03 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \cdot \text{day}
$$

---

**Step 4: Convert to Total Daily Human Dose (for a 60 kg adult)**

$$
\text{Total Daily Dose} = 0.03 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \cdot \text{day} \times 60 \, \text{kg} = 1.8 \, \text{mg/day}
$$

---

**Step 5: Final Interpretation**

$$
\boxed{\text{RfD} = 0.03 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \cdot \text{day} \approx 1.8 \, \text{mg/day for a 60 kg adult}}
$$

This RfD represents the **maximum safe daily exposure** over a lifetime. The actual clinical dose will be much lower because physicians must reserve a safety margin for sensitive subpopulations (children, pregnant women, elderly, hepatically/renally impaired patients).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Pre-clinical Development

The following analytical matrix helps decide **which pre-clinical studies are required** depending on the type of drug and intended clinical indication:

| Drug Class / Indication | Required Pre-clinical Package | Special Considerations |
|---|---|---|
| **Small molecule (oral, acute indication)** | 28-day + 90-day toxicity in rodent + non-rodent, genotoxicity battery, safety pharmacology core battery | Standard IND package |
| **Biologic (monoclonal antibody)** | 28-day + 6-month chronic toxicity in relevant species (often NHP), tissue cross-reactivity study | Immunogenicity assessment critical |
| **Oncology drug (cytotoxic)** | 28-day toxicity in 2 species, genotoxicity, **no requirement for carcinogenicity or reproductive studies** before Phase 1 | Patients have life-threatening disease, dose-finding is the priority |
| **Pediatric indication** | Juvenile animal studies in addition to adult | Age-specific developmental windows |
| **Drug for chronic indication (e.g., diabetes)** | 6-month + 12-month chronic toxicity, 2-year carcinogenicity in 2 species, reproductive battery | Long-term safety paramount |
| **Pregnancy Category X drug** | Full reproductive toxicology in 2 species, teratology | Contraindicated in pregnancy |
| **Gene therapy / Cell therapy** | Biodistribution, persistence, integration studies, immunogenicity, tumorigenicity | Special FDA/EMA guidelines apply |
| **Vaccine** | Local tolerance, repeat-dose toxicity, reproductive studies if applicable | Bridging studies for strain changes |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Drug Development Pipeline Success Rate Analysis

A pharmaceutical company screens **10,000 compounds** in early discovery.

**Step 1: Apply the typical attrition funnel:**

- Hits from primary screening: 100 (1% hit rate)
- Confirmed hits after secondary screening: 25 (25% of hits)
- Leads entering lead optimization: 10 (40% of confirmed hits)
- Candidate drugs entering pre-clinical development: 5 (50% of leads)
- IND candidates submitted to FDA: 2 (40% of pre-clinical candidates)
- Successful IND (FDA clearance to enter Phase 1): 2 (100%)
- Successful Phase 1 (safety): 1.5 (75%)
- Successful Phase 2 (proof of concept): 1 (67%)
- Successful Phase 3 (pivotal efficacy): 0.5 (50%)
- NDA / Marketing Approval: 0.5 (100%)

**Step 2: Calculate overall success rate**

$$
\text{Overall Success Rate} = \frac{0.5}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = 0.005\%
$$

This means **only 1 in 20,000 compounds** screened in early discovery eventually reaches the market — a stark illustration of why **rigorous pre-clinical selection** is so critical.

---

### 3.6 Mouse-to-Human Dose Conversion (Standard FDA Method) — Worked Example

**Problem:** A new anti-inflammatory drug **Inflammacure** showed efficacy in mice at 100 mg/kg. Calculate the human equivalent dose (HED).

**Step 1: Apply the FDA mouse-to-human conversion factor:**

$$
\text{HED (mg/kg)} = \text{Mouse Dose (mg/kg)} \times \frac{1}{12.3}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute and solve:**

$$
\text{HED (mg/kg)} = 100 \times \frac{1}{12.3}
$$

$$
\text{HED (mg/kg)} = \frac{100}{12.3} = 8.13 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 3: For a 70 kg adult:**

$$
\text{Absolute Dose} = 8.13 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{kg}} \times 70 \, \text{kg} = 569.1 \, \text{mg}
$$

**Step 4: Apply 1/10th safety factor for chronic dosing:**

$$
\text{Safe Starting Dose} = \frac{569.1}{10} = 56.91 \, \text{mg, rounded to } 50 \text{ or } 100 \, \text{mg}
$$

This is the typical range for an oral anti-inflammatory starting dose.
