# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Drug Testing (Bioanalytical Toxicology Screening)** refers to the systematic, scientifically validated process of detecting the presence of parent drug compounds, their metabolites, or specific biomarkers within a biological matrix obtained from a human subject. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology aligned with the **National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA)** and the **Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA)** guidelines, drug testing employs six principal biological matrices, namely oral fluid, urine, blood, hair, perspiration (sweat), and exhaled breath, each possessing unique pharmacokinetic windows, sensitivity profiles, and forensic admissibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Drug testing is broadly classified into **Presumptive Testing** (screening via immunoassays) and **Confirmatory Testing** (Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry, GC-MS, or Liquid Chromatography-Tandem Mass Spectrometry, LC-MS/MS). The six matrices differ in their **Detection Window** (the time period after drug use during which the drug or its metabolites can be detected).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — Detection Window vs. Window of Detection:**
> The **Detection Window** is highly matrix-dependent. While urine detects drug use from hours to days, hair can reveal drug history spanning **months to years**, making it invaluable in longitudinal forensic investigations and workplace drug history auditing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are a **forensic detective** investigating a crime scene. Different biological samples act like different "memory cards" recording the suspect's recent activities:

- **Blood** is like the **RAM** (Random Access Memory) of the body — it shows what is happening *right now* (real-time intoxication).
- **Urine** is like the **recent logbook** — it records drug use from the past 1–7 days, similar to a "recent activity" history.
- **Hair** is like a **hard drive archive** — it permanently stores the drug history for up to 90 days (or longer per cm of hair growth), akin to a permanent record.
- **Oral Fluid (Saliva)** is like a **recent chat history** — captures very recent use (last 5–48 hours) and is the easiest to collect non-invasively.
- **Perspiration (Sweat)** is like a **continuous background process log** — captured via a wearable patch that monitors drug presence over 7–14 days.
- **Breath (Breathalyzer)** is like a **real-time alert** — it instantly detects volatile substances (primarily ethanol) at the moment of testing.

## 1.3 The Six Biological Matrices — At a Glance

| # | Matrix | Primary Analyte(s) | Typical Detection Window | Standard Collection Method |
|---|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Oral Fluid** | Parent drug | 5 min – 48 h | Expectoration / Salivette swab |
| 2 | **Urine** | Drug + Metabolites | 2 h – 7 days (up to 30 days for cannabis) | Mid-stream clean-catch in sterile cup |
| 3 | **Blood** | Parent drug | 1 h – 24 h | Venipuncture (Vacutainer with anticoagulant) |
| 4 | **Hair** | Parent drug (incorporated into cortex) | 7 days – 90+ days (per cm growth) | Cut close to scalp, 100–120 strands |
| 5 | **Perspiration (Sweat)** | Parent drug + metabolites | 7 – 14 days continuous | **Sweat patch** (adhesive absorbent pad) |
| 6 | **Breath** | Ethanol (volatile compounds) | 0 – 12 h (acute intoxication) | Exhalation into fuel-cell/IR spectrometer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The clinical cutoff concentration for most immunoassay-based screening tests is typically **50 ng/mL** for cannabis metabolites, **1000 ng/mL** for opiates, amphetamines, and cocaine metabolites, as mandated by **SAMHSA** for federal workplace testing programs.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Matrix-by-Matrix Analysis

### 2.1.1 Oral Fluid (Saliva) Drug Testing

**Operational Principle:** Drugs and their metabolites diffuse from **plasma into saliva** primarily through passive diffusion across the acinar cells of the salivary glands. The concentration of a drug in saliva is roughly proportional to its free (unbound) fraction in plasma, since only the unbound drug crosses the salivary epithelium. This is described by the **Saliva-to-Plasma Ratio (S/P Ratio)**.

**Collection Procedure:**
1. Subject refrains from eating, drinking, or smoking for **at least 10 minutes** prior to collection.
2. A specialized absorbent collection device (e.g., **Quantisal™**, **Oral-Eze®**) is placed between the cheek and gum for 2–5 minutes.
3. The device is centrifuged, and the eluted oral fluid is analyzed via immunoassay and confirmed by **LC-MS/MS**.

**Key Analytical Features:**
- Detects **parent drug** rather than metabolites → indicates *recent* use.
- Detection window: typically **5–48 hours** post-ingestion.
- **Advantage:** Non-invasive, observed collection (minimal adulteration risk), rapid on-site screening possible.
- **Disadvantage:** Short detection window, limited sensitivity for cannabis due to low salivary THC concentrations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pharmaco-kinetic principle:** For basic drugs (e.g., amphetamines, cocaine), saliva is **acidified** (pH < 7) and trapping of drug occurs via "ion trapping" → salivary concentrations can exceed plasma concentrations.

### 2.1.2 Urine Drug Testing (UDT)

**Operational Principle:** After hepatic metabolism, drug metabolites are excreted via the **renal system** through glomerular filtration, tubular secretion, and passive reabsorption. Urine testing is the **gold standard** for workplace, military, athletic, and clinical drug screening programs.

**Specimen Collection Chain of Custody (CoC):**
1. **Donor verification** via photo ID.
2. Restroom with **no water access** to prevent dilution.
3. **Temperature check** of fresh specimen (must be **32–38°C within 4 minutes** of voiding).
4. **Adulterant strip test:** checks pH (4.5–8.0), specific gravity (1.003–1.035), creatinine (>20 mg/dL), and presence of oxidizing agents.
5. Split into **Bottle A (screening)** and **Bottle B (confirmatory)** for legally defensible testing.

**Key Analytical Features:**
- **Detection windows:**
  - Amphetamines: 1–4 days
  - Cocaine (benzoylecgonine metabolite): 2–4 days
  - Cannabis (THC-COOH): 3–30 days (chronic users)
  - Opiates: 1–3 days
  - PCP: 1–8 days
  - Benzodiazepines: 1–14 days (depending on half-life)
- **Advantage:** Highest sensitivity, large detection window, mature analytical methodology, lowest cost per test.
- **Disadvantage:** Easy adulteration, embarrassment of observed collection, doesn't indicate current impairment.

### 2.1.3 Blood Drug Testing

**Operational Principle:** Venous or capillary blood provides a **quantitative measurement of drug concentration** at the time of sampling. It represents the **gold standard for forensic impairment determination** because it directly correlates with the drug's concentration at the central nervous system (CNS) effect site.

**Collection Procedure:**
1. Venipuncture using a **Vacutainer® tube** containing sodium fluoride (preservative) and potassium oxalate (anticoagulant).
2. Sample stored at **2–8°C** and analyzed within **24 hours** (or frozen at **−20°C** for longer storage).
3. Analysis via **GC-MS** or **LC-MS/MS** for confirmation.

**Key Analytical Features:**
- **Detection window:** typically **1–24 hours** (short, due to rapid metabolism).
- **Advantage:** Quantitative, legally admissible for DUI/DUID cases, indicates *current* impairment.
- **Disadvantage:** Invasive (requires phlebotomist), short detection window, requires cold-chain logistics.

### 2.1.4 Hair Drug Testing

**Operational Principle:** Drugs and their metabolites are incorporated into the **hair shaft (medulla, cortex, cuticle)** via three mechanisms:
1. **Passive diffusion** from blood into the growing hair follicle cells.
2. **Deposition from sweat and sebum** onto the hair shaft.
3. **External contamination** (which is removed by wash procedures).

Since human scalp hair grows at approximately **1 cm per month** (0.3–0.4 mm/day), segmental analysis of hair strands can provide a **chronological history** of drug use.

**Collection Procedure:**
1. Cut **100–120 strands** of hair close to the scalp from the **vertex posterior region** (most uniform growth rate).
2. The root end is marked for orientation.
3. Hair is decontaminated via **organic solvent washes** (e.g., methanol, phosphate buffer).
4. Sample is **digested** (typically with NaOH, enzymes, or methanol), then analyzed via **GC-MS/MS or LC-MS/MS**.

**Key Analytical Features:**
- **Detection window:** **7 days to 90+ days** (depending on hair length, ~1 cm/month).
- **Advantage:** Longest detection window, non-invasive, difficult to adulterate, provides historical use pattern.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot detect very recent use (requires 5–7 days for incorporation into hair shaft), expensive, environmental contamination concerns.

### 2.1.5 Perspiration (Sweat) Drug Testing

**Operational Principle:** Drug molecules are excreted through **eccrine and apocrine sweat glands** via passive diffusion. A **sweat patch** (e.g., **PharmChek®** by PharmChem) is worn for 7–14 days, continuously absorbing insensible perspiration.

**Collection Procedure:**
1. The skin is cleaned with isopropyl alcohol to remove surface contamination.
2. An adhesive **collection patch** is applied to the upper arm or back.
3. After the wearing period, the patch is removed and the absorbent pad is eluted with buffer.
4. The eluate is screened via immunoassay and confirmed by GC-MS or LC-MS/MS.

**Key Analytical Features:**
- **Detection window:** **7–14 days** (entire wear period).
- **Advantage:** Continuous monitoring, tamper-resistant (patch is designed to show if removed), non-invasive.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot pinpoint *when* drug use occurred within the wear period, requires patient compliance, skin irritation possible.

### 2.1.6 Breathalyzer (Breath Alcohol Testing)

**Operational Principle:** Ethanol in alveolar blood equilibrates with alveolar air according to **Henry's Law** of gas-liquid partition. The **Blood-to-Breath Ratio (BBR)** of ethanol is **2100:1**, meaning **2100 mL of alveolar breath contains the same mass of ethanol as 1 mL of blood**.

**Analytical Methods:**
1. **Semiconductor Oxide Sensor:** Tin-oxide sensor whose electrical resistance changes with ethanol concentration (used in low-cost portable units).
2. **Fuel-Cell Sensor (Electrochemical):** Ethanol is oxidized at a platinum electrode, producing a measurable current proportional to concentration. Used in **evidential-grade** handheld breath testers (e.g., **Intoxilyzer®**, **DataMaster**).
3. **Infrared (IR) Spectroscopy:** IR absorption at specific wavelengths (3.4 µm for C-H stretch of ethanol) provides highly specific detection.
4. **Gas Chromatography (GC):** Laboratory-grade confirmation method.

**Operational Procedure (Standardized):**
1. Subject blows into the mouthpiece for **at least 6 seconds** of continuous exhalation to ensure **alveolar (deep-lung) air** sample.
2. The device measures breath alcohol concentration (BrAC) in **mg/L** or **g/210L**.
3. Result is converted to **Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC)** using the conversion factor.

**Legal Threshold (Driving Under the Influence, DUI):**
- In the United States: **0.08% BAC** (per se limit).
- In the European Union: **0.05% BAC** (most countries).
- In India (per Motor Vehicles Act): **30 mg/100 mL of blood** = **0.03% BAC**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Constant | Description | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|--------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Henry's Law:** $C_{blood} = k_H \cdot P_{breath}$ | Relates blood ethanol concentration to breath vapor pressure | $k_H$ is the Henry's constant for ethanol |
| 2 | **BAC Conversion:** $\text{BAC (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{BrAC (g/210 L)}}{1000} \times 100$ | Converts breath to blood alcohol | Result in % w/v |
| 3 | **Blood-to-Breath Ratio (BBR):** $\text{BAC (g/L)} = \text{BrAC (mg/L)} \div 2100$ | Standard ethanol partition ratio at 34°C | **2100:1** |
| 4 | **Widmark Equation:** $A = \text{BAC} \times m \times r$ | Total alcohol absorbed (g) | $m$ = body mass (kg), $r$ = Widmark factor (0.68 ♂, 0.55 ♀) |
| 5 | **Saliva-to-Plasma Ratio (S/P):** $\text{S/P} = \dfrac{C_{saliva}}{C_{plasma}}$ | Free drug fraction indicator | Drug-specific; >1 for basic drugs |
| 6 | **Cannabis Detection (urine):** $T_{detect} = f(t_{1/2}, \text{BMI}, \text{frequency})$ | Chronic users detectable up to **30 days** | THC-COOH cutoff: **50 ng/mL** |
| 7 | **Hair Growth Rate:** $L_{cm} = 0.3 \text{ mm/day} \times 30 \text{ days/month}$ | Approximate monthly growth | **~1 cm/month** |
| 8 | **Creatinine Normalization (urine):** $\text{CR}_{norm} = \dfrac{C_{drug}}{C_{creatinine}} \times 100$ | Adjusts for hydration variability | Expressed as ng/mg creatinine |
| 9 | **Fuel-Cell Nernst Equation:** $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Electrochemical sensor output | $n$ = electrons transferred (= 2 for ethanol) |
| 10 | **IR Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | Breath alcohol via IR absorption | $\varepsilon$ at 3.4 µm = molar absorptivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The **Widmark Equation** and the **Henry's Law partition ratio** are foundational in forensic toxicology software, used by law enforcement, traffic courts, and occupational health programs worldwide. The **S/P ratio** principle drives modern **therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM)** in clinical pharmacology.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Applications

- **Workplace Drug Testing Programs (WDT):** Pre-employment, random, post-accident, and return-to-duty testing.
- **Forensic Toxicology:** Post-mortem drug identification, DUI/DUID prosecution.
- **Sports Anti-Doping:** Governed by the **World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA)**, using **Athlete Biological Passport (ABP)**.
- **Pain Management Clinics:** Monitoring opioid compliance in chronic pain patients.
- **Criminal Justice:** Probation/parole monitoring, drug courts.
- **Insurance Underwriting:** Health and life insurance risk assessment.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Henry's Law Applied to Breath Alcohol

Henry's Law governs the equilibrium between a dissolved volatile compound in blood and its concentration in the alveolar air space:

$$
C_{blood} = k_H \cdot P_{alveolar}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the variables:**
- $C_{blood}$ = concentration of ethanol in blood (g/L)
- $P_{alveolar}$ = partial pressure of ethanol in alveolar air (mmHg)
- $k_H$ = Henry's Law constant (specific to ethanol at body temperature)

**Step 2 — Introduce the Blood-to-Breath Ratio (BBR):**
The empirical BBR is experimentally determined by comparing simultaneous blood and breath measurements. At a standard alveolar air temperature of **34°C** (the temperature of exhaled breath at the mouth), the BBR for ethanol is:

$$
\text{BBR} = \dfrac{C_{blood}}{C_{breath}} = 2100 : 1
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for breath-to-blood conversion:**
Given a measured breath alcohol concentration (BrAC) in grams per 210 liters of breath (the standard U.S. reporting unit), the equivalent BAC is:

$$
\text{BAC (g/dL)} = \dfrac{\text{BrAC (g/210 L)}}{2100 \times 100}
$$

This can be simplified by the standardized U.S. convention where the denominator's units of dL cancel, yielding BAC directly in **% w/v** (weight/volume percentage), where 1% BAC = 1 g ethanol per 100 mL blood.

**Step 4 — Converting to legal units:**
Since the legal limit in the U.S. is **0.08% BAC**, the equivalent breath concentration is:

$$
\text{BrAC} = 0.08 \times 2100 \times 100 = 16{,}800 \text{ µg/L} = 0.08 \text{ g/210 L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the BBR as **2100:1** unless otherwise specified. Some European countries use a different BBR (e.g., **2300:1** in Germany), which can affect legal interpretation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Widmark Equation for Alcohol Pharmacokinetics

The **Widmark Equation** (proposed by Erik M.P. Widmark in 1932) estimates the total amount of alcohol absorbed into the body from a measured BAC.

**Step 1 — Define the variables:**
- $A$ = total mass of alcohol in the body (grams)
- $\text{BAC}$ = blood alcohol concentration (%, or g/100 mL)
- $m$ = body mass (kg)
- $r$ = Widmark factor (dimensionless)

**Step 2 — State the equation:**

$$
A = \text{BAC} \times m \times r
$$

**Step 3 — Specify the Widmark factor:**
- For adult males: $r = 0.68$ (range 0.60–0.75)
- For adult females: $r = 0.55$ (range 0.45–0.60)

The lower $r$ for females reflects their typically higher body fat percentage, which does not distribute alcohol as effectively as lean tissue.

**Step 4 — Worked Example (typical KTU problem):**
A 75 kg male has a measured BAC of **0.10%**. Calculate the total mass of alcohol in his body.

$$
A = 0.10 \times 75 \times 0.68
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$
A = 0.10 \times 75 \times 0.68 = 5.10 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to standard drinks:**
In the U.S., one "standard drink" contains **14 g of pure ethanol**. Therefore:

$$
\text{Number of standard drinks} = \dfrac{5.10}{14} = 0.364 \text{ drinks}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Hair Growth-Based Detection Window

**Step 1 — State the growth rate:**

$$
\text{Growth Rate (G)} = 0.3 \text{ mm/day} \approx 1 \text{ cm/month}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine total length of detection window for a 3 cm hair segment:**

$$
L = G \times t
$$

$$
3 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ cm/month} \times 3 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 3 — Generalize the formula for any hair length:**

$$
t_{\text{months}} = L_{cm} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ month}}{1 \text{ cm}}
$$

For a typical 6 cm hair sample (cut close to the scalp), the detection window extends to:

$$
t = 6 \text{ cm} \times 1 \text{ month/cm} = 6 \text{ months}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Insight:** Hair analysis is **segmental** — the segment closest to the scalp represents the most recent month, while distal segments represent progressively older months. This enables forensic reconstruction of drug use history.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Parameter | Oral Fluid | Urine | Blood | Hair | Perspiration | Breathalyzer |
|-----------|------------|-------|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| **Detection Window** | 5 min – 48 h | 2 h – 7 days | 1 h – 24 h | 7 d – 90+ d | 7 – 14 d | 0 – 12 h |
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Non-invasive | Invasive (venipuncture) | Non-invasive | Non-invasive | Non-invasive |
| **Adulteration Risk** | Low | High | Very Low | Low | Very Low | Low (with observation) |
| **Cost per Test** | Moderate | Low | High | Very High | Moderate | Low – High |
| **Indicates Current Impairment?** | Yes (recent) | No | **Yes (gold standard)** | No (historical) | No (continuous) | **Yes (gold standard)** |
| **Forensic Admissibility** | Moderate | High | **Highest** | Moderate-High | Moderate | **Highest (DUI)** |
| **Sample Stability** | Hours (4°C) | Days (4°C) | Days (2–8°C) | Months/Years (RT) | Days (4°C) | Minutes (real-time) |
| **Primary Analyte** | Parent drug | Metabolite | Parent drug | Parent drug | Both | Parent (ethanol) |
| **Gold-Standard Confirmation** | LC-MS/MS | GC-MS | GC-MS | LC-MS/MS | GC-MS | GC, IR, Fuel-cell |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEBBT634, 2024 Scheme]**
**"Enumerate any three biological matrices used in drug testing and state one advantage of each."**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Urine:** *Advantage* — Has the longest established detection window (2–7 days for most drugs), mature analytical protocols, and is the gold standard for workplace testing.
2. **Blood:** *Advantage* — Provides quantitative measurement of parent drug, indicating current impairment; legally admissible for forensic DUI cases.
3. **Hair:** *Advantage* — Longest detection window (up to 90+ days per cm of growth), enabling retrospective analysis of drug use history.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEBBT634, 2024 Scheme]**
**"Explain the principle of a fuel-cell breathalyzer."**

**Model Answer:**
A **fuel-cell breathalyzer** is an electrochemical sensor used to detect ethanol in exhaled breath. The subject blows into the device, and the alveolar air passes over a **platinum electrode** immersed in an acidic electrolyte. Ethanol undergoes **oxidation** at the anode, producing **acetic acid, protons, and electrons**:

$$
C_2H_5OH + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3COOH + 4H^+ + 4e^-
$$

The electrons flow through an external circuit, generating a **current proportional to the ethanol concentration**. This current is measured and converted into a **Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC)** reading using the standard **2100:1** blood-to-breath ratio. Fuel-cell sensors are highly specific to ethanol and are used in **evidential-grade** breath testing instruments.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Pattern, PEBBT634]**
**"Discuss the principle, collection procedure, detection window, advantages, and limitations of urine drug testing. Compare it with hair drug testing."**

#### Part (a) — Urine Drug Testing (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
Urine drug testing (UDT) is based on the principle that drugs and their metabolites are excreted through the **renal system** following hepatic biotransformation. The parent drug undergoes Phase I (oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis) and Phase II (glucuronidation, sulfation) reactions, and the resulting metabolites are eliminated via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion into the urine. UDT typically detects **metabolites** rather than the parent drug, which is why it has a longer detection window than blood or oral fluid testing.

**Collection Procedure (Chain of Custody, CoC):**
1. **Donor verification** by photo identification and documentation.
2. **Privacy-restricted restroom** (no soap, no water access to prevent dilution).
3. **Mid-stream clean-catch** of 30–60 mL of urine in a sterile, tamper-evident container.
4. **Temperature verification** within 4 minutes of voiding (must be 32–38°C).
5. **Adulteration testing** via dipstick: checks pH (4.5–8.0), specific gravity (1.003–1.035), creatinine (>20 mg/dL), and absence of oxidizing agents (e.g., nitrite, bleach).
6. **Split sample** into Bottle A (screening) and Bottle B (confirmatory).

**Analytical Workflow:**
- **Screening:** Enzyme-Multiplied Immunoassay Technique (EMIT), Kinetic Interaction of Microparticles in Solution (KIMS), or Cloned Enzyme Donor Immunoassay (CEDIA). Cutoff: 50 ng/mL (cannabis), 1000 ng/mL (amphetamines, cocaine, opiates, PCP).
- **Confirmation:** Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS) is the gold standard for legally defensible results.

**Detection Windows (approximate):**
- Cannabis (THC-COOH): 3–30 days (single vs. chronic user)
- Cocaine (benzoylecgonine): 2–4 days
- Amphetamines/Methamphetamine: 1–4 days
- Opiates (morphine, codeine): 1–3 days
- Benzodiazepines: 1–14 days
- PCP: 1–8 days

**Advantages:**
- Mature, standardized methodology.
- High sensitivity with low cost.
- Large detection window compared to blood/oral fluid.
- Large body of legal precedent in forensic and workplace settings.

**Limitations:**
- High **adulteration risk** (substitution with synthetic urine, dilution, addition of nitrites).
- **Embarrassment** during observed collection.
- Indicates *prior* use, not current impairment.
- **Variability** due to hydration, renal function, and BMI.

#### Part (b) — Comparison with Hair Drug Testing (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Hair Testing Principle:**
Drugs and their metabolites are incorporated into the **hair shaft** via passive diffusion from blood during hair growth in the follicle, as well as from sebum and sweat secretions. The drug is permanently trapped within the **keratin matrix** of the cortex.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Urine | Hair |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Detection Window | 2 h – 30 days | 7 days – 90+ days |
| Indicates Current Use? | No (recent past) | No (historical) |
| Adulteration Risk | High | Low |
| Sample Collection | Invasive (privacy) | Non-invasive (hair cut) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Best Use Case | Workplace, athletic | Forensic history, rehab compliance |

**Advantages of Hair over Urine:**
- **Longer detection window** → can detect drug use months prior.
- **Difficult to adulterate** → more legally robust for historical cases.
- **Segmental analysis** → provides a chronological record of drug use (per cm of growth).

**Limitations of Hair:**
- Cannot detect **very recent use** (requires 5–7 days for hair shaft incorporation).
- **External contamination** concerns (cosmetic treatments, environmental exposure).
- **Ethnic bias:** differences in hair texture, porosity, and melanin content can affect drug binding.
- **Expensive** and requires specialized LC-MS/MS facilities.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Pattern, PEBBT634]**
**"Explain the principle, procedure, and forensic applications of breath alcohol analysis using a breathalyzer. Discuss the Henry's Law basis and calculate the BAC equivalent of a breath reading of 0.06 g/210 L."**

#### Part (a) — Breathalyzer Principle and Procedure (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
Breath alcohol analysis is based on the **Henry's Law** principle, which states that the concentration of a volatile compound dissolved in a liquid is proportional to its partial pressure in the gas phase above the liquid. **Ethanol** is a volatile, water-soluble compound that diffuses from arterial blood into the **alveolar air** of the lungs. The **Blood-to-Breath Ratio (BBR)** for ethanol at 34°C is **2100:1**, meaning 2100 mL of alveolar breath contains the same mass of ethanol as 1 mL of blood.

**Mathematical Foundation:**

$$
C_{blood} = k_H \cdot P_{breath}
$$

Where $k_H$ is the Henry's constant for ethanol at body temperature, and $P_{breath}$ is the partial pressure of ethanol vapor in exhaled breath.

**Analytical Methods:**
1. **Semiconductor (tin-oxide) sensor:** Low-cost, portable, but less specific.
2. **Fuel-cell electrochemical sensor:** Evidential-grade. Ethanol is oxidized at a platinum electrode, generating a current proportional to concentration.
3. **Infrared (IR) spectroscopy:** Uses the C-H stretch absorption of ethanol at 3.4 µm.
4. **Gas chromatography (GC):** Laboratory confirmation method, highest specificity.

**Standard Operating Procedure (DOT / NHTSA Protocol):**
1. **15-minute deprivation period** — Subject must not eat, drink, smoke, or regurgitate for 15 minutes before the test.
2. **Subject identification** by photo ID.
3. **Observation period** (typically 15–20 minutes) by a trained **Breath Alcohol Technician (BAT)** to ensure no belching, vomiting, or oral intake.
4. **First test (screening)** — Subject blows continuously for at least 6 seconds into the mouthpiece.
5. **Second test (confirmation)** — After a 2–3 minute waiting period, a second test is conducted.
6. The **lower** of the two readings is reported.

**Forensic Applications:**
- **Driving Under the Influence (DUI/DWI)** prosecution in traffic law.
- **Workplace post-accident testing.**
- **Aviation and maritime safety** (FAA, IMO standards).
- **Probation and parole** monitoring.
- **Alcohol rehabilitation program** compliance.

#### Part (b) — BAC Calculation from Breath Reading (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Breath Alcohol Concentration (BrAC) = **0.06 g/210 L**
- Blood-to-Breath Ratio (BBR) = **2100:1**

**Step 1 — Convert BrAC to g/L:**

$$
\text{BrAC} = 0.06 \text{ g/210 L} = \dfrac{0.06}{210} \text{ g/L} = 2.857 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/L}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mg/L (multiply by 1000):**

$$
\text{BrAC} = 0.2857 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the BBR conversion to obtain blood concentration:**

$$
\text{Blood ethanol (g/L)} = \dfrac{\text{BrAC (mg/L)}}{2100}
$$

$$
\text{Blood ethanol} = \dfrac{0.2857}{2100} = 1.361 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to % BAC (g/100 mL = g/dL):**

$$
\text{BAC (\%)} = 1.361 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 = 1.361 \times 10^{-2} \% = 0.01361\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

A breath reading of 0.06 g/210 L corresponds to a **BAC of approximately 0.0136%**, which is **well below** the U.S. legal per se DUI limit of 0.08% BAC. However, the subject would still exhibit measurable physiological effects (e.g., slight euphoria, reduced inhibitions) consistent with the early phase of alcohol absorption.

**Step 6 — Alternative direct conversion (faster method):**

$$
\text{BAC (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{BrAC (g/210 L)} \times 100}{2100 \times 100} = \dfrac{0.06 \times 100}{210{,}000} = 0.0000286\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction Note for examiners:** The standard U.S. formula is $\text{BAC (\%)} = \text{BrAC (g/210 L)} \times 0.0001$ approximately, but the **precise** derivation follows the steps above. Students should present the derivation, not the shortcut.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing g/210 L with g/100 mL:** The breath unit "g/210 L" is sometimes confused with g/dL of blood. Students must explicitly state the BBR conversion step.
> 2. **Forgetting the 34°C normalization:** The BBR of 2100:1 applies at 34°C (alveolar air temperature at the mouth). At higher body temperatures, the ratio decreases.
> 3. **Ignoring the two-test protocol:** The U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) requires **two consecutive tests** separated by a 2-minute waiting period. Forgetting this loses marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning residual mouth alcohol:** The 15-minute deprivation period exists to eliminate false positives from mouth alcohol. Students often omit this critical step.
> 5. **Confusing fuel-cell with semiconductor sensors:** Fuel-cell sensors are **ethanol-specific** (interference from acetone, methane, isopropanol is minimal), whereas semiconductor sensors are **non-specific** and prone to false positives.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Six biological matrices:** Oral fluid, Urine, Blood, Hair, Perspiration (sweat), Breath. Each has a unique **detection window** ranging from minutes (breath) to months (hair).

- **Urine is the gold standard** for routine workplace testing, but cannot indicate current impairment. Detection window: **2 h to 30 days** (cannabis).

- **Blood is the gold standard for forensic impairment** (DUI), with detection window **1–24 h** and quantitative measurement of parent drug.

- **Hair testing** uses the principle of drug incorporation into the keratin matrix during hair growth (~1 cm/month). Provides **90+ days** of historical use data.

- **Oral fluid** detects **parent drug** (not metabolites), reflecting very recent use. Collection is via specialized absorbent device (e.g., Salivette, Quantisal).

- **Sweat patch** is a 7–14 day continuous monitoring device, ideal for compliance programs.

- **Breathalyzer** works on **Henry's Law** with BBR = **2100:1**. Three sensor types: **semiconductor** (cheap, non-specific), **fuel-cell** (evidential grade, ethanol-specific), **IR** (laboratory, highly specific).

- **SAMHSA-5 drug panel** for federal workplace testing: Cannabis, Cocaine, Amphetamines, Opiates, PCP. Cutoff for cannabis metabolite (THC-COOH) is **50 ng/mL**.

- **Confirmatory test** is always **GC-MS** or **LC-MS/MS**. **Presumptive screen** uses immunoassays (EMIT, KIMS, CEDIA).

- **Widmark Equation:** $A = \text{BAC} \times m \times r$, where $r = 0.68$ (males), $0.55$ (females). Used to estimate total body alcohol load.

- **Chain of Custody (CoC):** Mandatory for forensic samples. Includes donor ID, collection witness, tamper-evident seals, temperature check, and two-bottle split (A for screening, B for confirmation).

- **Creatinine normalization** in urine (ng drug per mg creatinine) adjusts for sample dilution; a ratio <20 mg/dL suggests **dilute specimen**.

- **Hair segmental analysis** = chronological record; segment closest to scalp = most recent month.

- **S/P Ratio (Saliva-to-Plasma):** For basic drugs, S/P > 1 due to **ion trapping** in acidic saliva.

- **Adulteration markers in urine:** abnormal pH, low creatinine, low specific gravity, presence of nitrite, chromium(VI), glutaraldehyde, pyridinium chlorochromate.

- **Legal BAC limits:** U.S. = **0.08%**, EU = **0.05%**, India = **0.03%**.

- **Detection window order (shortest to longest):** Breath < Blood < Oral fluid < Urine < Sweat patch < Hair.

- **KTU exam mantra:** Always pair a method with its **(a) Principle, (b) Procedure, (c) Detection window, (d) Advantages, (e) Limitations** — this is the universal 14-mark question template for Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques Used for Drug Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT634 - CLINICAL RESEARCH AND DRUG DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques Used for Drug Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drug Testing** encompasses the systematic, scientifically rigorous, and ethically regulated methodologies employed to evaluate the **safety, efficacy, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and toxicological profile** of a drug candidate before, during, and after its approval for human or veterinary use. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, drug testing is broadly classified into **Preclinical Testing** (in vitro and in vivo laboratory studies) and **Clinical Testing** (human trials under regulatory supervision).

The drug testing pipeline forms the **backbone of pharmaceutical R&D** and is governed by strict international guidelines such as:
- **ICH (International Council for Harmonisation)** guidelines (e.g., ICH M3, ICH S9, ICH E6-GCP)
- **OECD Principles of Good Laboratory Practice (GLP)**
- **Schedule Y** of the Indian Drugs and Cosmetics Act
- **US FDA 21 CFR Part 312**
- **EMA (European Medicines Agency)** directives

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The School Exam System for a New Medicine"**
>
> Imagine a student (the *new drug candidate*) who claims to be the "best problem-solver in the world." Before granting a degree (market approval), the university (regulatory authority) makes the student pass several levels of exams:
>
> 1. **Homework & Lab tests** → *Preclinical in vitro tests* (test-tube and cell-based assays)
> 2. **Mock tests with a panel of experienced mentors** → *Preclinical in vivo tests* (animal model studies)
> 3. **Small classroom trial** → *Phase I Clinical Trial* (20-100 healthy volunteers for safety)
> 4. **Department-wide trial** → *Phase II Clinical Trial* (100-300 patients for efficacy)
> 5. **University-wide trial** → *Phase III Clinical Trial* (1000-3000 patients for comparative efficacy)
> 6. **Alumni feedback after graduation** → *Phase IV Pharmacovigilance* (post-marketing surveillance)
>
> Only after passing *each level* does the student earn the right to be used by the public. Drug testing follows the same stepwise, fail-at-any-stage principle.

### 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks (Bolded for KTU Examination Recall):**
> - **No Observable Adverse Effect Level (NOAEL)**: Highest dose at which **no** statistically or biologically significant increase in adverse effects is observed. Typically expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day**.
> - **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50)**: Dose that kills **50%** of the test animal population. Unit: **mg/kg**.
> - **ED₅₀ (Effective Dose 50)**: Dose that produces the therapeutic response in **50%** of subjects. Unit: **mg/kg**.
> - **Therapeutic Index (TI)** = **LD₅₀ / ED₅₀** — a *safety margin* indicator.
> - **Cmax**: Peak plasma concentration (ng/mL or µg/mL).
> - **Tmax**: Time to reach Cmax (hours).
> - **AUC (Area Under the Curve)**: Total drug exposure over time (ng·h/mL).
> - **Bioavailability (F)**: Fraction of administered drug reaching systemic circulation, **0 ≤ F ≤ 1**.
> - **IC₅₀**: Concentration required to inhibit **50%** of a biological process in vitro (µM or nM).
> - **EC₅₀**: Concentration producing **50%** of maximal effect.

### 1.4 Broad Classification of Drug Testing Techniques

Drug testing techniques are organized into a hierarchical, gated pipeline:

| # | Stage | Test Type | Key Output |
|---|-------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | Discovery | In vitro biochemical assays | Target validation, IC₅₀ |
| 2 | Discovery | Cell-based assays | Cellular EC₅₀ |
| 3 | Preclinical | In vivo animal studies (rodent/dog/primate) | PK/PD, NOAEL, LD₅₀ |
| 4 | Preclinical | Genotoxicity & safety pharmacology | Mutagenicity, cardiotoxicity |
| 5 | Clinical | Phase I, II, III, IV trials | Safety, efficacy, post-market surveillance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of *PEBBT634* places special emphasis on understanding the *techniques* (methods, instruments, and assays) rather than just the regulatory framework. Mastery of the *flow of a drug candidate from bench to bedside* is essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Layers of Drug Testing

Drug testing is performed in **five progressive tiers**, each with increasing complexity, cost, and regulatory scrutiny:

### Tier 1: In Vitro Biochemical Assays
- **Purpose**: Test drug–target interaction in isolation.
- **Common Techniques**:
  - **Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA)**
  - **Radioligand Binding Assays**
  - **Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR)**
  - **Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC)**
  - **Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET)**
- **Output**: Binding affinity (Kd), inhibition constant (Ki), IC₅₀.

### Tier 2: In Vitro Cell-Based Assays
- **Purpose**: Evaluate drug effect on living cells.
- **Common Techniques**:
  - **MTT / MTS Assay** (cell viability)
  - **Flow Cytometry** (apoptosis, cell cycle)
  - **Reporter Gene Assays**
  - **Patch-Clamp Electrophysiology**
  - **Western Blot / qPCR** (downstream signaling)
- **Output**: Cellular EC₅₀, mechanism of action, cytotoxicity.

### Tier 3: In Vivo Animal Studies
- **Purpose**: Assess whole-organism PK/PD and toxicology.
- **Common Animal Models**:
  - **Rodents** (mice, rats) — most common; cheap, fast reproduction.
  - **Dogs (Beagle)** — second species for regulatory requirement.
  - **Non-human primates (NHPs)** — used only when no alternative exists.
- **Routes of Administration**: Oral (p.o.), intravenous (i.v.), intraperitoneal (i.p.), subcutaneous (s.c.).
- **Output**: ADME profile, NOAEL, LD₅₀, target organ toxicity.

### Tier 4: ADME / Pharmacokinetic Studies
- **A — Absorption**: Measured by F (bioavailability) and Caco-2 permeability.
- **D — Distribution**: Measured by Volume of Distribution (Vd).
- **M — Metabolism**: Studied via liver microsomes, S9 fractions, CYP450 isoform profiling.
- **E — Excretion**: Renal clearance (CLr), biliary excretion, half-life (t½).

### Tier 5: Clinical Trials
- **Phase I**: 20–100 healthy volunteers; safety, dose-finding, MTD (Maximum Tolerated Dose).
- **Phase II**: 100–300 patients; efficacy, dose-response.
- **Phase III**: 1,000–3,000 patients; comparative efficacy, large-scale safety.
- **Phase IV**: Post-marketing pharmacovigilance; rare adverse event detection.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Examination**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Therapeutic Index | $\text{TI} = \dfrac{LD_{50}}{ED_{50}}$ | dimensionless | Safety margin |
| 2 | Henderson-Hasselbalch | $pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | dimensionless | Ionization, oral absorption |
| 3 | Bioavailability (Oral) | $F = \dfrac{AUC_{oral} \cdot D_{iv}}{AUC_{iv} \cdot D_{oral}}$ | fraction (0–1) | Extent of absorption |
| 4 | Volume of Distribution | $V_d = \dfrac{\text{Total Dose}}{C_0}$ | L or L/kg | Tissue distribution |
| 5 | Clearance | $CL = k_e \cdot V_d = \dfrac{\text{Rate of elimination}}{C_{ss}}$ | mL/min or L/h | Drug elimination rate |
| 6 | Half-life | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_e}$ | hours | Dosing interval |
| 7 | AUC (trapezoidal rule) | $AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{(C_i + C_{i+1})(t_{i+1} - t_i)}{2}$ | ng·h/mL | Total exposure |
| 8 | Michaelis-Menten Kinetics | $v = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | varies | Enzyme inhibition/induction |
| 9 | Hill Equation | $E = \dfrac{E_{max} \cdot [D]^n}{EC_{50}^n + [D]^n}$ | % effect | Dose-response curves |
| 10 | Schild Equation | $pA_2 = \log_{10}(DR - 1) - \log_{10}[B]$ | M | Receptor antagonism potency |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **Henderson-Hasselbalch equation** and **Therapeutic Index** are *guaranteed* to appear in some form in the 14-mark Part B questions. Memorize them with correct units.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Pharmaceutical Engineering

- **Drug Repurposing** (e.g., remdesivir for COVID-19) relies on high-throughput *in vitro* screening against target panels.
- **In silico ADME prediction** (using software like Schrödinger, ADMETlab) is used to *prioritize* molecules before wet-lab testing, saving approximately **$1.5–2.0 million per failed candidate**.
- **Organ-on-chip** microfluidic devices are emerging as the next-generation *in vitro* testing platform, replacing animal models in select applications.
- **PBPK (Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic) modeling** integrates in vitro data to predict in vivo behavior, reducing late-stage attrition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lipinski's Rule of Five** (used in early-stage drug-likeness filtering — *not* a physical testing technique, but a key decision gate before testing):
> - Molecular weight ≤ **500 Da**
> - Log P ≤ **5**
> - H-bond donors ≤ **5**
> - H-bond acceptors ≤ **10**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Therapeutic Index (TI) and Margin of Safety

The **Therapeutic Index (TI)** quantifies the *separation* between a drug's effective dose and its lethal dose. A high TI indicates a *safer* drug.

### Step 1: Define the Lethal Dose 50 (LD₅₀)

By definition, LD₅₀ is the dose at which **50%** of the test population dies. Empirically, this is obtained from a *log-dose vs. % mortality* probit plot.

$$ LD_{50} = \text{antilog}\left[\log_{10}(D) \text{ at probit } = 5 \right] $$

### Step 2: Define the Effective Dose 50 (ED₅₀)

Similarly, ED₅₀ is the dose producing the desired therapeutic response in 50% of subjects.

$$ ED_{50} = \text{antilog}\left[\log_{10}(D) \text{ at 50\% max effect} \right] $$

### Step 3: Form the Ratio

$$ \text{TI} = \dfrac{LD_{50}}{ED_{50}} $$

### Step 4: Practical Example Calculation

Suppose an experimental antihypertensive compound has:
- LD₅₀ (rat, oral) = **500 mg/kg**
- ED₅₀ (rat, blood pressure reduction) = **5 mg/kg**

$$ \text{TI} = \dfrac{500 \text{ mg/kg}}{5 \text{ mg/kg}} = 100 $$

A TI of 100 is considered **excellent** (e.g., penicillin has a TI in the millions; warfarin has a TI ≈ 2, hence its narrow safety margin).

### Step 5: Margin of Safety (Alternative, more conservative)

$$ \text{Margin of Safety} = \dfrac{LD_{1}}{ED_{99}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** For drugs with steep dose-response curves (small difference between ED₅₀ and LD₅₀), the **Margin of Safety** is preferred over TI because it uses *lethal dose in 1%* and *effective dose in 99%* — capturing the worst-case tail risk.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bioavailability (F) from AUC Data

### Step 1: Define AUC
AUC = Area Under the Plasma Concentration vs. Time Curve, typically computed by the **linear trapezoidal rule**.

### Step 2: Derive the Formula

For any drug, the *amount reaching systemic circulation* equals *Dose × F*. This amount is also proportional to AUC (since AUC ∝ amount absorbed):

$$ AUC = \dfrac{F \cdot D}{CL} $$

For the same drug given by two different routes (e.g., oral and IV):

$$ AUC_{iv} = \dfrac{F_{iv} \cdot D_{iv}}{CL} = \dfrac{1 \cdot D_{iv}}{CL} \quad (\text{since } F_{iv} = 1) $$

$$ AUC_{oral} = \dfrac{F_{oral} \cdot D_{oral}}{CL} $$

### Step 3: Solve for F (oral)

Dividing the two equations and rearranging:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\dfrac{AUC_{oral}}{AUC_{iv}} &= \dfrac{F_{oral} \cdot D_{oral}}{D_{iv}} \\[8pt]
F_{oral} &= \dfrac{AUC_{oral} \cdot D_{iv}}{AUC_{iv} \cdot D_{oral}}
\end{aligned} $$

### Step 4: Worked Example

A new analgesic is administered to 12 healthy volunteers in a crossover study:
- IV: 100 mg → AUC = 5,000 ng·h/mL
- Oral: 200 mg tablet → AUC = 4,500 ng·h/mL

$$ \begin{aligned}
F &= \dfrac{4{,}500 \cdot 100}{5{,}000 \cdot 200} \\[8pt]
  &= \dfrac{450{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\[8pt]
  &= 0.45 \text{ or } 45\%
\end{aligned} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Only 45% of the orally administered dose reaches systemic circulation. The other 55% is lost to *first-pass metabolism* in the liver, gut-wall metabolism, or incomplete absorption.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Drug Testing Techniques

| Technique | Stage | Sample Type | Time Required | Cost (Relative) | Information Gained |
|-----------|-------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| ELISA | Preclinical | Protein lysate | 4–6 h | Low | Quantitative antigen/antibody |
| MTT Assay | Preclinical | Cultured cells | 24–48 h | Low | Cell viability % |
| Flow Cytometry | Preclinical | Cells in suspension | 1–2 h | Medium | Cell cycle, apoptosis |
| Western Blot | Preclinical | Cell/tissue lysate | 24–48 h | Medium | Protein expression |
| qPCR | Preclinical | RNA extract | 4–6 h | Medium | Gene expression |
| Radioligand Binding | Preclinical | Purified receptor | 4–8 h | High | Kd, Ki, Bmax |
| SPR (Biacore) | Preclinical | Purified protein | 30 min/sample | High | Real-time kinetics |
| Animal PK Study | Preclinical | Plasma, tissues | 1–4 weeks | Very High | ADME, t½, Cmax, Tmax |
| Acute Toxicity (OECD 425) | Preclinical | Whole animal | 14 days | High | LD₅₀, NOAEL |
| Ames Test | Preclinical | Bacterial culture | 48–72 h | Medium | Mutagenicity |
| Phase I Trial | Clinical | Healthy human | 6–18 months | Very High | MTD, safety |
| Phase III Trial | Clinical | Patient population | 3–5 years | Extreme | Efficacy, comparative |
| PBPK Modeling | Translational | Computational | Days–weeks | Medium | Whole-body PK prediction |

---

## 3.4 Derivations: Pharmacokinetic Steady-State & Multiple Dosing

### Step 1: Derive Css (Steady-State Plasma Concentration)

For repeated dosing at interval τ, at steady state the *rate in = rate out*:

$$ \text{Rate in} = \dfrac{F \cdot D}{\tau} $$

$$ \text{Rate out} = CL \cdot C_{ss} $$

Equating:

$$ \dfrac{F \cdot D}{\tau} = CL \cdot C_{ss} $$

Solving:

$$ C_{ss} = \dfrac{F \cdot D}{CL \cdot \tau} $$

### Step 2: Time to Reach Steady State

The time to reach steady state depends only on the **elimination half-life**, not the dose or dosing interval:

$$ t_{ss} \approx 4 \text{ to } 5 \times t_{1/2} $$

### Step 3: Worked Example

A drug has t½ = 8 h. After how long will steady state be reached?

$$ t_{ss} = 5 \times 8 = 40 \text{ hours} \approx 1.67 \text{ days} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write that steady state depends on the dose. It does **not** — only the half-life matters. A higher dose gives a *higher* Css but does *not* change the time to reach it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Drug Testing Pipeline — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drug Discovery: Target Identification] --> B[In Vitro Biochemical Assays ELISA, SPR, FRET]
    B --> C[Hit Identification IC50 Determination]
    C --> D[Lead Optimization Medicinal Chemistry]
    D --> E[In Vitro Cell-Based Assays MTT, Flow Cytometry]
    E --> F[Preclinical In Vivo Studies Rodent, Beagle]
    F --> G[ADME Pharmacokinetic Profiling]
    G --> H[Toxicology Studies NOAEL, LD50, Genotoxicity]
    H --> I{IND Filing Regulatory Decision}
    I -->|Approved| J[Phase I Safety 20-100 Volunteers]
    I -->|Rejected| Z[Compound Discontinued]
    J --> K[Phase II Efficacy 100-300 Patients]
    K --> L[Phase III Large-Scale 1000-3000 Patients]
    L --> M{NDA Filing}
    M -->|Approved| N[Phase IV Post-Marketing Pharmacovigilance]
    M -->|Rejected| Z
    N --> O[Drug on Market Continuous Monitoring]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each node is a *gated* decision point. Approximately **<10%** of compounds entering preclinical testing reach the market. The arrows represent forward progression; the **Rejection → Discontinuation** pathway is statistically the most-traveled route.

## 4.2 In Vitro vs In Vivo — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InVitro["In Vitro Testing"]
        direction TB
        A1[Purified Target Protein] --> A2[Biochemical Assay]
        A3[Cell Lines] --> A4[Cell-Based Assay]
        A5[3D Organoids] --> A6[Microphysiological System]
    end
    subgraph InVivo["In Vivo Testing"]
        direction TB
        B1[Rodent Mouse Rat] --> B2[PK Study]
        B3[Beagle Dog] --> B4[Toxicology]
        B5[Non-Human Primate] --> B6[Confirmatory PK]
    end
    InVitro -->|Data Integration| C[Decision Gate IND Enabling Package]
    InVivo --> C
    C --> D[Regulatory Submission]
```

## 4.3 Clinical Trial Phases — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Compound Selected for Development] --> P1
    subgraph Phase1["Phase I - Safety"]
        P1[20-100 Healthy Volunteers]
    end
    Phase1 --> P2sub
    subgraph Phase2["Phase II - Efficacy and Dose"]
        P2sub[100-300 Patients Target Indication]
    end
    Phase2 --> P3sub
    subgraph Phase3["Phase III - Comparative and Large-Scale"]
        P3sub[1000-3000 Patients Multi-Center]
    end
    Phase3 --> R{NDA MAA Approval Decision}
    R -->|Approved| P4sub
    subgraph Phase4["Phase IV - Pharmacovigilance"]
        P4sub[Post-Market Surveillance Thousands of Patients]
    end
    R -->|Rejected| PEnd[Compound Shelved]
```

## 4.4 ADME Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drug Administration] --> B[A Absorption Gut Caco-2]
    B --> C[Systemic Circulation]
    C --> D[D Distribution Tissues Vd]
    D --> E[M Metabolism Liver CYP450]
    E --> F[E Excretion Kidney Bile]
    F --> G[Eliminated from Body]
    C --> H[Pharmacological Target]
    H --> I[Pharmacodynamic Effect]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
